Feather (feat. Cise Starr & Akin) — Nuja: The quintessential Nujabes track — jazz piano samples layered over boom-bap beats with Cise Starr's effortless flow. If Daikanyama had a theme song, this would be it.
aruarian dance — Nujabes: From the Samurai Champloo soundtrack, this instrumental is pure lo-fi jazz-hop meditation. The kind of track that makes staring out a window feel cinematic.
81Summer — Uyama Hiroto: Nujabes' longtime collaborator channeling that same jazz-hop warmth with flute and keys. Named after a Japanese summer and it absolutely sounds like one.
SHINE feat.DIVINITY — Shuya Okino: Kyoto Jazz Massive co-founder Shuya Okino blending deep house with soulful vocals. Sophisticated Tokyo club music for daytime listening.
Eclipse — Kyoto Jazz Massive: Japanese deep house royalty delivering a lush, jazzy house cut that's been a Shibuya staple for decades. Warm, organic, and impossibly smooth.
Luv Letter — DJ Okawari: Piano-driven instrumental hip-hop that floats like cherry blossoms. DJ Okawari carries the Nujabes torch with grace — perfect focus music.
Momma's Groove (Jimpster Mix) — Osunlade: Jimpster strips Osunlade's spiritual house down to a deep, rolling groove that just breathes. The kind of deep house that makes a café feel like a low-key club.
Porchlight And Rocking Chairs (KiNK Remi: Bulgarian producer KiNK adds his signature live-hardware energy to Jimpster's deep house. Warm, textured, and subtly propulsive — ideal work-alongside music.
Can You Feel It — Larry Heard: Mr. Fingers himself delivering one of deep house's foundational tracks. Those dreamy pads basically invented an entire genre — timeless doesn't even cover it.
Rain — Kerri Chandler: Kerri Chandler's deep house is like a warm bath for your ears. Jersey's finest bringing soulful keys and hypnotic grooves — pure mood.
Shades of Jae — Moodymann: Detroit's Moodymann at his most soulful, chopping jazz samples over dusty house beats. It's got that lived-in quality that makes deep house feel like home.
Falling Up (Carl Craig Remix) — Theo Par: Two Detroit legends colliding — Carl Craig takes Theo Parrish's raw material and sculpts it into something cosmic. Deep, driving, and endlessly listenable.
Plastic Love — Mariya Takeuchi: The city pop anthem that conquered the algorithm. Takeuchi's bittersweet disco-funk hits differently in a Daikanyama café — you're literally in the city it soundtracks.
Stay With Me — Miki Matsubara: Matsubara's midnight groove went from forgotten 80s single to global phenomenon. Silky city pop with a melancholy undercurrent — perfect for late afternoon focus.
Blue Lagoon — Masayoshi Takanaka: Takanaka's fusion guitar work is pure sunshine energy — tropical funk that sounds like a vacation. The palette cleanser this playlist needs right in the middle.
I Can't Stop — Mark de Clive-Lowe: NZ-born, LA-based, Tokyo-connected — MdCL bridges jazz improvisation with electronic production like nobody else. A broken-beat gem with serious groove.
Canopée — Polo & Pan: French duo Polo & Pan crafting dreamy tropical house with analog warmth. It sounds like sunlight filtering through trees, which is basically the T-Site courtyard vibe.
GLOWED UP — KAYTRANADA: Kaytranada and Anderson .Paak making deep house you can rap over. That production is so clean it practically shimmers — peak cool without trying.
South of the River — Tom Misch: South London's Tom Misch with breezy jazz-guitar-meets-electronic-soul. Laid-back but intricate — the kind of musicianship that rewards close listening or works as background.
Vibin' Out — FKJ: French multi-instrumentalist FKJ layering live loops into a warm, jazzy groove. It feels like watching someone build a song in real time — organic and effortless.
Rolling into One — Jordan Rakei: New Zealand-born, London-based Rakei blending neo-soul with electronic textures. His voice is velvet and the production is meticulous — headphone music at its finest.
Ghost Town — Kanye West: Kanye at his most vulnerable, with 070 Shake's outro turning the whole thing into something transcendent. A beautiful, raw moment that hits harder in a quiet café.
Ojitos Lindos — Bad Bunny: Bad Bunny's tender side — a reggaetón love song with Bomba Estéreo that's genuinely sweet. The perfect closer, like the sun setting over T-Site as you pack up your laptop.
